title: Dog Priapism Guide: Emergency Care & Triage for Persistent Erection
meta_desc: Priapism in dogs is a high-level medical emergency. Learn what to do if your dog has a persistent erection and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
What is Priapism in Dogs?

Priapism is a medical condition characterized by a persistent, involuntary erection that lasts for an extended period—typically longer than 30 minutes—without any sexual stimulation. It occurs when blood flows into the penis but cannot drain back out, leading to painful swelling and potential damage to the sensitive tissues.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

Priapism is a critical veterinary emergency. Because the blood trapped in the penis is deoxygenated, the tissue can quickly become damaged. If left untreated for more than a few hours, the tissue may begin to die (necrosis), which can lead to permanent dysfunction, severe infection, or the need for surgical amputation. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you observe this condition.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact a veterinary professional, they may ask for a clear photo of the affected area. Visual evidence allows the triage team to assess the color of the tissue (looking for signs of purple or black discoloration), the severity of the swelling, and whether there is any visible trauma or discharge. This information helps the clinical team prioritize your dog's case and prepare the necessary equipment for immediate treatment upon your arrival.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if the swelling goes down on its own?
faqs.answer: No. Priapism is time-sensitive. Waiting even a few hours can lead to irreversible tissue death and permanent damage. Seek emergency care immediately.

faqs.question: What causes priapism in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include neurological disorders, spinal cord injuries, certain medications, local trauma, or underlying infections.

faqs.question: How is priapism treated by a vet?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves sedation, the application of cold compresses, specialized lubricants, or medications to reduce swelling. In severe cases, a veterinarian may need to manually drain the blood or perform surgery.
